section.title: By Faith
section.sectionRationale: Having said the righteous will live by faith, the writer now parades the great cloud of witnesses, from creation to the prophets, who lived and died by faith. Each acted on God's word about things unseen, longing for a better, heavenly country. Together they show what faith is and does, yet they did not receive the promise apart from us, for God had planned something better in Christ.
section.thesis: Faith is the assurance of what is hoped for and the conviction of what is not seen; from Abel to the prophets, the ancients were commended for such faith, obeying God's word, enduring suffering, and longing for a better country, yet none received the promise in full, for God planned that only together with us, in Christ, would they be made perfect.
section.frameNote: Six movements: the definition of faith; the faith of the first ancestors, Abel, Enoch, and Noah; the faith of Abraham and Sarah; the faith of the patriarchs and Moses; faith that conquered and faith that suffered; and the better thing God planned for all His people together.

verses.text: Now {{pistis|faith}} is the {{hypostasis|assurance}} of what we hope for and the {{elenchos|certainty}} of what we do not see.
This is why the ancients were {{martyreo|commended}}.
By faith we understand that the universe was formed at God’s command, so that what is seen was not made out of what was visible.
By faith Abel offered God a better sacrifice than Cain did. By faith he was commended as righteous when God gave approval to his gifts. And by faith he still speaks, even though he is dead.
By faith Enoch was taken up so that he did not see death: “He could not be found, because God had taken him away.” For before he was taken, he was commended as one who pleased God.
And without faith it is impossible to please God. For anyone who approaches Him must believe that He exists and that He rewards those who earnestly seek Him.
By faith Noah, when warned about things not yet seen, in godly fear built an ark to save his family. By faith he condemned the world and became heir of the righteousness that comes by faith.

units.label: Abraham and Sarah
units.note: looking for the city with foundations

verses.text: By faith Abraham, when called to go to a place he would later receive as his inheritance, obeyed and went, without knowing where he was going.
By faith he dwelt in the promised land as a stranger in a foreign country. He lived in tents, as did Isaac and Jacob, who were heirs with him of the same promise.
For he was looking forward to the city with foundations, whose architect and builder is God.
By faith Sarah, even though she was barren and beyond the proper age, was enabled to conceive a child, because she considered Him faithful who had promised.
And so from one man, and he as good as dead, came descendants as numerous as the stars in the sky and as countless as the sand on the seashore.

All these people died in faith, without having received the things they were promised. However, they saw them and welcomed them from afar. And they acknowledged that they were strangers and exiles on the earth.
Now those who say such things show that they are seeking a country of their own.
If they had been thinking of the country they had left, they would have had opportunity to return.
Instead, they were longing for a better country, a heavenly one. Therefore God is not ashamed to be called their God, for He has prepared a city for them.
By faith Abraham, when he was tested, offered up Isaac on the altar. He who had received the promises was ready to offer his one and only son,
even though God had said to him, “Through Isaac your offspring will be reckoned.”
Abraham reasoned that God could raise the dead, and in a sense, he did receive Isaac back from death.
By faith Isaac blessed Jacob and Esau concerning the future.
By faith Jacob, when he was dying, blessed each of Joseph’s sons and worshiped as he leaned on the top of his staff.
By faith Joseph, when his end was near, spoke about the exodus of the Israelites and gave instructions about his bones.
By faith Moses’ parents hid him for three months after his birth, because they saw that he was a beautiful child, and they were unafraid of the king’s edict.
By faith Moses, when he was grown, refused to be called the son of Pharaoh’s daughter.
He chose to suffer oppression with God’s people rather than to experience the fleeting enjoyment of sin.
He valued disgrace for Christ above the treasures of Egypt, for he was looking ahead to his reward.
By faith Moses left Egypt, not fearing the king’s anger; he persevered because he saw Him who is invisible.
By faith he kept the Passover and the sprinkling of blood, so that the destroyer of the firstborn would not touch Israel’s own firstborn.

units.label: Faith that conquered and faith that suffered
units.note: the world was not worthy of them

verses.text: By faith the people passed through the Red Sea as on dry land; but when the Egyptians tried to follow, they were drowned.
By faith the walls of Jericho fell, after the people had marched around them for seven days.
By faith the prostitute Rahab, because she welcomed the spies in peace, did not perish with those who were disobedient.
And what more shall I say? Time will not allow me to tell of Gideon, Barak, Samson, Jephthah, David, Samuel, and the prophets,
who through faith conquered kingdoms, administered justice, and gained what was promised; who shut the mouths of lions,
quenched the raging fire, and escaped the edge of the sword; who gained strength from weakness, became mighty in battle, and put foreign armies to flight.
Women received back their dead, raised to life again. Others were tortured and refused their release, so that they might gain a better resurrection.
Still others endured mocking and flogging, and even chains and imprisonment.
They were stoned, they were sawed in two, they were put to death by the sword. They went around in sheepskins and goatskins, destitute, oppressed, and mistreated.
The world was not worthy of them. They wandered in deserts and mountains, and hid in caves and holes in the ground.
These were all commended for their faith, yet they did not receive what was promised.
God had planned something better for us, so that together with us they would be made perfect.

pistis.gloss: Faith, trust that takes God at His word. The keyword of the chapter, repeated as a refrain: 'by faith' each person acted on God's promise about things unseen, and by such faith they were commended and lived.

hypostasis.gloss: Assurance, substance, the firm ground under something. Faith gives present substance and reality to what is hoped for, so that the future promise becomes a settled confidence on which one can stand and act.

elenchos.gloss: Conviction, proof, certainty. Faith is the inner certainty and evidence of realities that cannot be seen, treating God's unseen promises as sure and proven because God has spoken.

martyreo.gloss: To bear witness, to testify, and in the passive to be commended or well attested. The ancients were commended by God through their faith; Scripture bears witness to them, and their example bears witness to us.

basic.context: After saying the righteous live by faith, the writer shows what that looks like across the whole Old Testament. He defines faith, then walks through hero after hero, from Abel to the prophets, each acting on God's word about things they could not yet see. Some conquered kingdoms; others were tortured and killed. All were commended for their faith, yet none received the full promise in their lifetime, because God planned that they and we would be made complete together in Christ.
basic.hermeneutics: Read the repeated 'by faith' as the drumbeat of the chapter. Faith here is not vague optimism but taking God at His word about unseen realities and acting on it. The list is selective and moves toward its climax: they looked for a better, heavenly country, and were not made perfect apart from us.
basic.originalLanguages: Faith, pistis, is the hypostasis, assurance or substance, of what we hope for and the elenchos, conviction, of what we do not see. By such faith the ancients were martyreō, commended, attested by God.
basic.typology: Many episodes foreshadow Christ: Abel's better sacrifice, Isaac received back as from death, the Passover blood, the better resurrection. Moses valued disgrace for Christ, showing the ancients' faith was ultimately faith in the coming Redeemer.
basic.christ: The faith of all these witnesses was oriented, however dimly, toward the promise fulfilled in Christ. Moses esteemed disgrace for Christ; all died looking for what only Christ brings, and only in Him are they and we made perfect together.
basic.god: God rewards those who earnestly seek Him and is not ashamed to be called the God of these pilgrims, for He has prepared a city for them. God planned something better, that all His people across the ages would be perfected together.
basic.crossReferences: Genesis records Abel, Enoch, Noah, Abraham, and Sarah. Exodus tells of Moses and the Passover. Joshua recounts Jericho and Rahab. Hebrews 12 draws the application.
basic.oneStory: The whole Old Testament is a story of people living and dying by faith in promises they did not see fulfilled; that story reaches its goal in Christ, in whom the promise comes and all the faithful are made perfect together.

context.literary: The chapter is a sustained example list, a rhetorical device that builds by the anaphora 'by faith'. It flows from the definition in verses 1 to 3, through chronological examples, to a crescendo of suffering saints, and concludes with the theological point in verses 39 to 40 that ties directly into chapter 12.
context.historical: The examples span Israel's whole history, from the primeval accounts of Genesis to the persecutions of the prophets and, in verses 35 to 38, likely the sufferings of the Maccabean era. The catalogue would powerfully encourage readers facing their own pressure to give up.
context.canonical: The chapter offers the New Testament's fullest definition and demonstration of faith, reading the Old Testament saints as models of trust in God's promises, and its climax insists that the old covenant faithful and the new covenant church share one destiny in Christ.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To define faith and to encourage wavering believers by the example of the whole company of the faithful, showing that faith trusts God's unseen promises, endures suffering, and looks to the better country God has prepared in Christ.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The chapter describes the faith of the ancients, but its purpose is deeply prescriptive by example: the readers are to imitate this faith, as chapter 12 makes explicit with the image of the great cloud of witnesses.

debates.question: Is the faith of the Old Testament saints the same faith that saves under the new covenant?

views.label: One faith, differently informed
views.case: The ancients trusted God's promises about the unseen, looking forward to what Christ would bring, while believers now trust the same God in the light of Christ's finished work; it is one saving faith with a shared object, the God of the promise.

views.label: Faith oriented to the promise, fulfilled in Christ
views.case: Their faith reached toward realities not yet revealed, and verse 40 shows they are made perfect only together with us in Christ, so their faith and ours meet in the one Redeemer to whom all the promises pointed.

debates.bearing: Either way the chapter unites the whole people of God in one faith and one destiny, so that the readers stand in a long, honoured line of those who trust God's word.

study.originalLanguages: The chapter opens with a definition built on two weighty words. Faith, pistis, is the hypostasis of what we hope for, its substance or firm ground, that which gives future promises present reality; and it is the elenchos, the conviction or proof, of things not seen, treating the unseen as certain because God has spoken. By exactly this faith, the writer says, the ancients were martyreō, commended, borne witness to by God Himself and recorded in Scripture. The refrain 'by faith' then repeats down the chapter, and the same verb of commendation returns in verse 39: these were all attested through their faith, yet did not receive the promise apart from us.

typology.fulfillment: Points to the better sacrifice of Christ, whose blood speaks a better word than Abel's.

typology.type: Isaac offered and received back as from death
typology.fulfillment: Foreshadows the death and resurrection of the Son whom the Father did not spare.

typology.type: The Passover and the sprinkling of blood
typology.fulfillment: Fulfilled in Christ our Passover, whose blood shields His people from judgment.

typology.type: The pilgrims longing for a heavenly city
typology.fulfillment: Fulfilled in the city God has prepared, the heavenly Jerusalem entered through Christ.

study.christ: Christ is the hidden center of the chapter, the one toward whom all this faith was reaching. The ancients trusted God's promises about unseen realities, and those promises find their yes in Christ. Strikingly, Moses is said to have valued disgrace for Christ above the treasures of Egypt, showing that faith in the coming Redeemer animated even the old covenant saints. They all died without receiving the promise, and verse 40 reveals why: God planned something better, so that only together with us, in Christ, would they be made perfect. Christ is the fulfillment that completes the faith of every age.
study.god: God is the object and reward of faith throughout the chapter. He is the Creator who formed the universe by His word, the One who must be believed to exist and to reward those who earnestly seek Him. He is faithful, so Sarah could trust Him who promised, and He is not ashamed to be called the God of these wandering pilgrims, for He has prepared a city for them. Above all, God is the planner of history who purposed something better, that all His people across the ages would be perfected together in Christ, uniting the whole company of the faithful in one grand design.

crossReferences.note: Abraham believed God and it was credited to him as righteousness, the pattern of faith.
The righteous will live by faith, the text that launches this demonstration of faith.
The great cloud of witnesses and Jesus the author and perfecter of faith, the chapter's application.
The testing of Abraham and the offering of Isaac, behind verses 17 to 19.

study.oneStory: The Old Testament, read as one story, is a long procession of people who staked everything on God's word about things they could not see. They obeyed, endured, conquered, and suffered, and they died still waiting, pilgrims longing for a better country. Hebrews gathers them into a single great cloud of witnesses and reveals the point of the whole story: God planned that they and we, the faithful of every age, would be made perfect together in Christ, in whom all the promises they trusted finally come true.

quiz.q: How does the chapter define faith?

options.text: The assurance of what we hope for and the conviction of what we do not see.
A vague hope that things will work out.
options.note: Faith here is settled assurance grounded in God's word, not vague optimism.

options.text: Belief only in what can be proven by sight.
options.note: Faith is precisely the conviction of what is not seen.

options.text: Confident trust that God will keep His promises.
options.note: This is true and near the mark; the precise definition is the assurance of what we hope for and the conviction of what we do not see.

quiz.q: What did the pilgrims of faith long for, according to verses 13 to 16?

options.text: A better country, a heavenly one, the city God has prepared.
options.note: They sought a heavenly homeland, not the land they had left.

options.text: A homeland and city of their own.
options.note: True, they sought a country of their own, which the passage specifies is a better, heavenly one that God has prepared.

options.text: Wealth and comfort in this life.
options.note: They confessed they were strangers and exiles, longing for the heavenly city.
Their longing was for God's prepared city, not political power.

quiz.q: What surprising thing did faith lead some to endure, in verses 35 to 38?

options.text: Torture, mocking, imprisonment, and death, refusing release for a better resurrection.
options.note: Faith led not only to victories but to great suffering.

options.text: Great victories such as conquering kingdoms and shutting lions' mouths.
options.note: Faith did bring such triumphs in verses 33 to 34, but the surprising point is that the same faith also led others to torture and death.

options.text: A quiet life free of any hardship.
options.note: Many suffered greatly; the world was not worthy of them.
Some escaped by faith, but others endured suffering and death by the same faith.

quiz.q: Why did none of the ancients receive the promise in full?

options.text: Because God planned something better, that they and we would be made perfect together in Christ.
Because the promise awaited its fulfillment in Christ.
options.note: This is right in substance; verse 40 explains that God planned for them and us to be made perfect together in Christ.
God planned something better; He did not forget.

options.text: Because the promise was never real.
options.note: The promise is real and is fulfilled in Christ, uniting them with us.

questions: Faith is the assurance of what we hope for. Where is God asking you to act on His word about something you cannot yet see?
The pilgrims confessed they were strangers and exiles longing for a better country. How does living for the heavenly city change your grip on this world's comforts?
Faith led some to great victories and others to great suffering. How does this guard you against thinking faith always brings an easy life?
Moses valued disgrace for Christ above the treasures of Egypt. What treasures are you tempted to prize above faithfulness to Christ?
The faithful of every age are made perfect together with us in Christ. How does belonging to this great company encourage you to keep believing?

translationNotes.note: 'Assurance' renders hypostasis, which can mean substance or firm confidence; faith gives present reality to future hopes. 'Certainty' renders elenchos, conviction or proof of the unseen.
'Disgrace for Christ' reads the Messiah into Moses' experience; the reproach the people of God bore anticipated the reproach borne by and for the Christ to come.
'Sawed in two' reflects a tradition about the death of Isaiah; the catalogue of sufferings in verses 35 to 38 likely includes the martyrdoms of the Maccabean period.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched from bible.helloao.org for Hebrews 11:1-40. No em dashes occurred in this passage. Sermon set to none; no expositor series confirmed this session.
